---
title: "Tumor Burden Model: Introduction & Biology"
author:
  - Vijay Ivaturi
execute:
  cache: true
  error: false
julia: 
  exeflags: ["+Pumas@2.7.1"]
format:
  html:
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        <style>
          .header-container {
            width: 100%;
          }
          .logo {
            display: block;
            width: 150px;
            margin-left: -2.5%;
          }
        </style>
        <link rel = "shortcut icon" href = "https://pumas.ai/favicon.ico" />
    include-before-body:
      text: |
        <div class="header-container">
          <img src="https://pumas-assets.s3.amazonaws.com/CompanyLogos/PumasAI/RGB/PNG/Pumas+AI+Primary%404x.png" alt="Pumas Logo" class="logo">
        </div>
    self-contained-math: true
    anchor-sections: true
    theme: default
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 4
    toc-expand: 2
    toc-location: left
    toc-title: Contents
    number-sections: true
    code-summary: Show/Hide Code
embed-resources: true
fig-format: svg
fig-width: 8
fig-height: 6
bibliography: references.bib
csl: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/citation-style-language/styles/master/apa.csl
---

:::{.callout-note}
## Tumor Burden Tutorial Series

This is **Tutorial 1 of 6** in the Tumor Burden series.

| # | Tutorial | Status |
|---|----------|--------|
| 1 | **Model Introduction (current)** | You are here |
| 2 | [Copula Vpop Generation](tb_02_copula_vpop_tutorial.qmd) | |
| 3 | [Global Sensitivity Analysis](tb_03_gsa_tutorial.qmd) | |
| 4 | [Structural Identifiability](tb_04_structural_identifiability_tutorial.qmd) | |
| 5 | [MILP Calibration](tb_05_milp_calibration_tutorial.qmd) | |
| 6 | [VCT Simulation](tb_06_vct_simulation_tutorial.qmd) | |
:::

## Learning Outcomes

This tutorial introduces the Tumor Burden Model, a foundational example for understanding In Silico Clinical Trials (ISCTs). The model describes tumor dynamics during cancer chemotherapy and will be used throughout this tutorial series [@cortes2025; @qi2023].

In this tutorial, you will learn:

- The clinical context: non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) chemotherapy
- The biological basis of tumor response: treatment-sensitive vs resistant cells
- The mathematical model equation and what each parameter represents
- How to build a Pumas model using the `@model` macro
- How to simulate typical patient dynamics
- How to visualize treatment vs control scenarios

## Background

### Clinical Context: NSCLC Chemotherapy

Non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) is the most common type of lung cancer, accounting for approximately 85% of all lung cancer cases. Chemotherapy remains a cornerstone of treatment, but patient responses vary dramatically:

- Some patients achieve **complete response** (tumor disappears)
- Some achieve **partial response** (tumor shrinks significantly)
- Others experience **progressive disease** (tumor grows despite treatment)

**The central question:** What determines which patients respond to treatment?

:::{.callout-tip}
## From Observation to Model

Researchers observed that tumors often show a characteristic **biphasic response** to chemotherapy:

1. **Initial shrinkage:** Chemotherapy kills treatment-sensitive cells
2. **Eventual regrowth:** Treatment-resistant cells proliferate

This observation suggests a simple but powerful model: tumors contain two distinct cell populations with different drug sensitivities.
:::

### The Two-Population Hypothesis

The tumor burden model posits that a tumor consists of two cell populations:

```{mermaid}
flowchart TB
    subgraph tumor["Tumor at Diagnosis"]
        direction LR
        sens["Treatment-Sensitive Cells<br/>(fraction f)"]
        res["Treatment-Resistant Cells<br/>(fraction 1-f)"]
    end

    sens -->|"Under treatment"| sens_fate["Die at rate k"]
    res -->|"Always"| res_fate["Grow at rate g"]

    sens_fate --> outcome1["Tumor shrinks initially"]
    res_fate --> outcome2["Tumor regrows eventually"]

    style sens fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000
    style res fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#00cc00
    style outcome1 fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#01579b
    style outcome2 fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100
```

**Biological interpretation:**

| Population | Symbol | Under Treatment | Biological Basis |
|:-----------|:-------|:----------------|:-----------------|
| **Sensitive cells** | f | Die at rate k | Express drug targets, susceptible to chemotherapy |
| **Resistant cells** | 1-f | Grow at rate g | Pre-existing mutations, drug efflux pumps, stem-like properties |

This simple model captures the essence of why some patients respond better than others: **patients with a higher fraction of sensitive cells (high f) will show better initial response**.

## Libraries

Load the necessary packages for this tutorial.

```{julia}
#| output: false
using Pumas
using DataFrames
using LogExpFunctions         # logit, logistic transformations
using AlgebraOfGraphics
using CairoMakie
using Statistics
using Random
```

**What each package does:**

- `Pumas`: Pharmacometric modeling and simulation
- `LogExpFunctions`: Numerically stable logit and logistic transformations
- `AlgebraOfGraphics` + `CairoMakie`: Publication-quality visualizations

## Part 1: The Model Equation

### Mathematical Formulation

The tumor burden model describes the total tumor size $N(t)$ as the sum of two exponential terms [@qi2023]:

$$N(t) = N_0 \cdot f \cdot e^{-k \cdot t} + N_0 \cdot (1-f) \cdot e^{g \cdot t}$$

Where:

| Symbol | Parameter | Units | Description |
|:-------|:----------|:------|:------------|
| $N_0$ | Initial tumor | normalized | Baseline tumor size (set to 1) |
| $f$ | Sensitive fraction | unitless | Fraction of treatment-sensitive cells (0 to 1) |
| $k$ | Death rate | d⁻¹ | Rate at which sensitive cells die under treatment |
| $g$ | Growth rate | d⁻¹ | Rate at which resistant cells proliferate |
| $t$ | Time | days | Time since treatment start |

:::{.callout-note}
## Understanding the Equation

Let's break down the two terms:

**First term:** $N_0 \cdot f \cdot e^{-k \cdot t}$ — Sensitive cell population

- Starts at $N_0 \cdot f$ (fraction f of the initial tumor)
- Decays exponentially with rate k when treated
- Eventually approaches 0 as sensitive cells are eliminated

**Second term:** $N_0 \cdot (1-f) \cdot e^{g \cdot t}$ — Resistant cell population

- Starts at $N_0 \cdot (1-f)$ (the remaining fraction)
- Grows exponentially with rate g
- Eventually dominates the total tumor

**Net effect:** Initial shrinkage (first term dominates) followed by regrowth (second term dominates).
:::

### Parameter Values from Literature

The parameter values come from @qi2023, who fit this model to NSCLC clinical trial data:

```{julia}
# Population (typical) parameter values
typical_f = 0.27    # 27% of tumor is treatment-sensitive
typical_g = 0.0013  # Resistant cells grow ~0.13% per day
typical_k = 0.0091  # Sensitive cells die ~0.91% per day

# Inter-individual variability (omega values on transformed scale)
omega_f = 2.16  # High variability in sensitive fraction
omega_g = 1.57  # Moderate variability in growth rate
omega_k = 1.24  # Moderate variability in death rate

# Critical correlation from the data
cor_f_g = -0.64  # Negative correlation: high f ↔ low g

println("Tumor Burden Model Parameters")
println("=" ^ 50)
println("Typical values (population medians):")
println("  f (sensitive fraction): $typical_f ($(round(typical_f*100, digits=1))%)")
println("  g (growth rate): $typical_g d⁻¹")
println("  k (death rate): $typical_k d⁻¹")
println("\nInter-individual variability (ω on transformed scale):")
println("  ωf = $omega_f")
println("  ωg = $omega_g")
println("  ωk = $omega_k")
println("\nCorrelation: r(f, g) = $cor_f_g")
```

:::{.callout-tip}
## Why the Negative Correlation?

The correlation r(f, g) = -0.64 is biologically meaningful:

**Patients with more sensitive cells (high f) tend to have slower-growing resistant cells (low g).**

Possible explanations:

1. **Tumor heterogeneity:** Aggressive tumors may have both fewer sensitive cells AND faster-growing resistant cells
2. **Evolutionary pressure:** In highly proliferative tumors, resistance mechanisms may be more prevalent
3. **Stem cell fraction:** Resistant cells may correlate with cancer stem cell populations

This correlation is crucial for realistic virtual population generation (Tutorial 2).
:::

## Part 2: Building the Pumas Model

### Model Structure Overview

A Pumas model is defined using the `@model` macro with several blocks:

```{mermaid}
flowchart TB
    param["@param<br/>Population parameters"]
    random["@random<br/>Inter-individual variability"]
    cov["@covariates<br/>External inputs (treatment)"]
    pre["@pre<br/>Individual parameter calculations"]
    init["@init<br/>Initial conditions"]
    dynamics["@dynamics<br/>ODE system"]
    derived["@derived<br/>Observable outputs"]

    param --> pre
    random --> pre
    cov --> pre
    pre --> init
    pre --> dynamics
    init --> dynamics
    dynamics --> derived

    style param fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0
    style random fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0
    style dynamics fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32
    style derived fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100
```

### Complete Model Definition

```{julia}
tumor_burden_model = @model begin
    @metadata begin
        desc = "Tumor Burden Model for NSCLC Chemotherapy"
        timeu = u"d"  # Time unit is days
    end

    @param begin
        # Typical values (population parameters)
        tvf ∈ RealDomain(lower=0.0, upper=1.0, init=0.27)
        tvg ∈ RealDomain(lower=0.0, init=0.0013)
        tvk ∈ RealDomain(lower=0.0, init=0.0091)

        # Inter-individual variability (diagonal covariance matrix)
        # Values on the transformed scale (logit for f, log for g and k)
        Ω ∈ PDiagDomain(init=[2.16, 1.57, 1.24])

        # Residual error (standard deviation)
        σ ∈ RealDomain(lower=0.0, init=0.05)
    end

    @random begin
        # Random effects vector (one per parameter)
        η ~ MvNormal(Ω)
    end

    @covariates treatment

    @pre begin
        # Transform random effects to individual parameters
        # f: logit-normal transformation (bounded 0-1)
        f = logistic(logit(tvf) + η[1])

        # g, k: log-normal transformation (positive values)
        g = tvg * exp(η[2])
        k = tvk * exp(η[3])

        # Treatment effect on death rate
        # treatment = 0 → control arm (no killing)
        # treatment = 1 → treatment arm (killing at rate k)
        k_eff = treatment * k

        # Initial conditions (normalized tumor = 1)
        N_sens_0 = f           # Sensitive cells
        N_res_0 = 1.0 - f      # Resistant cells
    end

    @init begin
        N_sens = N_sens_0
        N_res = N_res_0
    end

    @dynamics begin
        # Sensitive cells: die at effective rate (0 if control, k if treatment)
        N_sens' = -k_eff * N_sens

        # Resistant cells: always grow at rate g
        N_res' = g * N_res
    end

    @derived begin
        # Total normalized tumor size
        Nt = @. N_sens + N_res

        # Observation with residual error
        tumor_size ~ @. Normal(Nt, σ)
    end
end

println("Model defined successfully!")
```

### Understanding Each Block

Let's examine each model block in detail.

#### The `@param` Block

```{julia}
#| echo: false
println("@param Block: Population Parameters")
println("=" ^ 50)
println("""
Parameters defined:
  tvf ∈ RealDomain(lower=0.0, upper=1.0)  → Typical value of f (bounded)
  tvg ∈ RealDomain(lower=0.0)             → Typical value of g (positive)
  tvk ∈ RealDomain(lower=0.0)             → Typical value of k (positive)
  Ω ∈ PDiagDomain(init=[...])             → Diagonal covariance matrix
  σ ∈ RealDomain(lower=0.0)               → Residual error SD
""")
```

:::{.callout-note}
## Domain Types

| Domain | Constraints | Use Case |
|:-------|:------------|:---------|
| `RealDomain()` | Any real number | Unbounded parameters |
| `RealDomain(lower=0.0)` | Positive only | Rates, clearances |
| `RealDomain(lower=0.0, upper=1.0)` | Between 0 and 1 | Fractions, bioavailability |
| `PDiagDomain(n)` | Positive diagonal matrix | Variance-covariance |
:::

#### The `@random` Block

```{julia}
#| echo: false
println("@random Block: Inter-Individual Variability")
println("=" ^ 50)
println("""
η ~ MvNormal(Ω)

This creates a vector of 3 random effects:
  η[1] → affects f (sensitive fraction)
  η[2] → affects g (growth rate)
  η[3] → affects k (death rate)

η values are sampled from N(0, Ω) for each virtual patient.
""")
```

#### The `@pre` Block: Parameter Transformations

The `@pre` block contains the **key transformations** that convert population parameters and random effects into individual parameters.

```{julia}
#| echo: false
println("@pre Block: Individual Parameter Calculation")
println("=" ^ 50)
println("""
For parameter f (bounded 0-1):
  f = logistic(logit(tvf) + η[1])

  Step 1: logit(tvf) transforms 0.27 to the unbounded scale
          logit(0.27) = log(0.27/0.73) ≈ -0.995

  Step 2: Add random effect η[1] (can be any real number)

  Step 3: logistic(...) transforms back to (0, 1)

For parameters g and k (positive values):
  g = tvg * exp(η[2])

  This is log-normal: when η[2] = 0, g = tvg (typical)
                      when η[2] > 0, g > tvg
                      when η[2] < 0, g < tvg
""")
```

:::{.callout-important}
## Why These Transformations?

The transformations ensure:

1. **Parameters stay in valid ranges:**
   - f always between 0 and 1 (logit-normal)
   - g and k always positive (log-normal)

2. **Symmetric variability on the transformed scale:**
   - A patient with η = +1 is as "far from typical" as one with η = -1

3. **Realistic distributions:**
   - Log-normal for rates (right-skewed, common in PK/PD)
   - Logit-normal for fractions (appropriate for bounded parameters)
:::

#### The `@dynamics` Block

```{julia}
#| echo: false
println("@dynamics Block: ODE System")
println("=" ^ 50)
println("""
N_sens' = -k_eff * N_sens
  → Sensitive cells decrease exponentially when treated
  → k_eff = 0 (control) means no change
  → k_eff = k (treatment) means exponential decay

N_res' = g * N_res
  → Resistant cells always grow exponentially
  → Independent of treatment

These two ODEs implement the mathematical model:
  N(t) = N_0 * f * e^(-k*t) + N_0 * (1-f) * e^(g*t)
""")
```

## Part 3: Simulating a Typical Patient

### Setting Up the Simulation

Let's simulate a "typical" patient (η = 0) receiving treatment.

```{julia}
# Population parameters
pop_params = (
    tvf = 0.27,
    tvg = 0.0013,
    tvk = 0.0091,
    Ω = [2.16 0.0 0.0; 0.0 1.57 0.0; 0.0 0.0 1.24],
    σ = 0.05
)

# Observation times (weekly for 18 weeks)
obs_times = collect(0.0:7.0:126.0)

# Create a subject for the treatment arm
subject_treatment = Subject(
    id = 1,
    covariates = (treatment = 1,),  # Treatment arm
    observations = (tumor_size = nothing,),
    time = obs_times
)

# Create a subject for the control arm
subject_control = Subject(
    id = 2,
    covariates = (treatment = 0,),  # Control arm
    observations = (tumor_size = nothing,),
    time = obs_times
)

println("Subjects created:")
println("  Treatment subject: $(subject_treatment.id), treatment = 1")
println("  Control subject: $(subject_control.id), treatment = 0")
println("  Observation times: $(length(obs_times)) weekly measurements")
```

### Simulating the Typical Patient

```{julia}
# For a typical patient, η = [0, 0, 0]
# This gives individual parameters equal to population typical values
typical_randeffs = (η = [0.0, 0.0, 0.0],)

# Simulate treatment arm
sim_treatment = simobs(
    tumor_burden_model,
    subject_treatment,
    pop_params,
    typical_randeffs
)

# Simulate control arm
sim_control = simobs(
    tumor_burden_model,
    subject_control,
    pop_params,
    typical_randeffs
)

# Convert to DataFrames
df_treatment = DataFrame(sim_treatment)
df_control = DataFrame(sim_control)

# Add arm labels
df_treatment.arm .= "Treatment"
df_control.arm .= "Control"

println("Simulation completed!")
println("\nTypical patient parameters (η = 0):")
println("  f = $(pop_params.tvf)")
println("  g = $(pop_params.tvg) d⁻¹")
println("  k = $(pop_params.tvk) d⁻¹")
```

### Visualizing Treatment vs Control

```{julia}
#| fig-cap: "Tumor dynamics for a typical patient: treatment (blue) vs control (red). Treatment causes initial tumor shrinkage as sensitive cells die, followed by regrowth as resistant cells proliferate. Control arm shows only growth."

# Combine data for plotting
combined_df = vcat(df_treatment, df_control)

# Add reference lines as data
ref_lines = DataFrame(
    threshold = ["Baseline", "Complete Response"],
    value = [1.0, 0.14]
)

# Create plot using AlgebraOfGraphics
plt = data(combined_df) *
    mapping(:time => "Time (days)", :Nt => "Normalized Tumor Size", color = :arm) *
    visual(Lines, linewidth = 3)

# Draw the plot
fig = Figure(size = (800, 500))
ax = Axis(fig[1, 1], title = "Typical Patient: Treatment vs Control")

# Draw main lines with custom colors
ag = draw!(ax, plt, scales(Color = (; palette = [:steelblue, :red])))

# Add reference lines
hlines!(ax, [1.0], color = :gray, linestyle = :dash, linewidth = 1)
hlines!(ax, [0.14], color = :green, linestyle = :dot, linewidth = 2)

# Add annotations near actual curve positions
text!(ax, 45, 0.88, text = "Initial response\n(sensitive cells dying)",
    fontsize = 11, align = (:center, :top))
text!(ax, 115, 0.98, text = "Regrowth\n(resistant cells)",
    fontsize = 11, align = (:center, :bottom))

# Legend outside the plot
legend!(fig[1, 2], ag, framevisible = false, tellheight = false)

fig
```

:::{.callout-note}
## Interpreting the Curves

**Treatment arm (blue):**

1. **Days 0-60:** Tumor shrinks as treatment kills sensitive cells (27% of initial tumor)
2. **Days 60-126:** Tumor regrows as resistant cells (73% of initial tumor) continue proliferating
3. **Nadir (minimum):** Occurs when the declining sensitive population equals the growing resistant population

**Control arm (red):**

1. No killing of any cells
2. Both populations grow at their respective rates (but f doesn't affect control since k_eff = 0)
3. Exponential growth dominated by the larger resistant population
:::

## Part 4: Understanding the Dynamics

### Analytical Solution

For deeper understanding, let's examine the analytical solution:

```{julia}
# Analytical solution function
function tumor_analytical(t, f, g, k; treatment=true)
    k_eff = treatment ? k : 0.0
    # N(t) = f * exp(-k_eff * t) + (1-f) * exp(g * t)
    return f * exp(-k_eff * t) + (1 - f) * exp(g * t)
end

# Calculate key metrics for typical patient
f, g, k = 0.27, 0.0013, 0.0091

# Time to nadir (minimum tumor size)
# Occurs when dN/dt = 0: -k*f*exp(-k*t) + g*(1-f)*exp(g*t) = 0
# Solving: t_nadir = ln(k*f / (g*(1-f))) / (k + g)
t_nadir = log(k * f / (g * (1 - f))) / (k + g)

# Tumor size at nadir
N_nadir = tumor_analytical(t_nadir, f, g, k)

# Final tumor size (week 18)
N_final = tumor_analytical(126.0, f, g, k)

println("Tumor Dynamics Analysis")
println("=" ^ 50)
println("\nTypical patient (f=$f, g=$g, k=$k):")
println("  Time to nadir: $(round(t_nadir, digits=1)) days ($(round(t_nadir/7, digits=1)) weeks)")
println("  Tumor size at nadir: $(round(N_nadir, digits=3)) ($(round((1-N_nadir)*100, digits=1))% reduction)")
println("  Tumor size at week 18: $(round(N_final, digits=3))")

# Would this patient achieve "complete response"?
cr_threshold = 0.14
achieved_cr = N_nadir < cr_threshold
println("\n  Complete response (< $cr_threshold)? ", achieved_cr ? "Yes" : "No")
```

### Sensitivity to Parameters

How do the model outputs change with different parameter values?

```{julia}
#| fig-cap: "Effect of sensitive fraction (f) on tumor dynamics. Higher f leads to deeper initial response and longer time to regrowth."

# Create DataFrame for all f values
t_vec = collect(0:1:126)
f_values = [0.10, 0.27, 0.50, 0.80]

# Build DataFrame with all trajectories
sensitivity_df = DataFrame()
for f_val in f_values
    N = [tumor_analytical(ti, f_val, g, k) for ti in t_vec]
    temp_df = DataFrame(
        time = t_vec,
        tumor_size = N,
        f_label = "f = $f_val ($(round(Int, f_val*100))% sensitive)"
    )
    append!(sensitivity_df, temp_df)
end

# Create plot using AlgebraOfGraphics
plt = data(sensitivity_df) *
    mapping(:time => "Time (days)",
            :tumor_size => "Normalized Tumor Size",
            color = :f_label => "Sensitive Fraction") *
    visual(Lines, linewidth = 2.5)

# Draw the plot
fig = Figure(size = (900, 500))
ax = Axis(fig[1, 1], title = "Effect of Sensitive Fraction (f) on Tumor Dynamics")

ag = draw!(ax, plt, scales(Color = (; palette = [:red, :orange, :steelblue, :green])))

# Add reference lines
hlines!(ax, [1.0], color = :gray, linestyle = :dash, linewidth = 1)
hlines!(ax, [0.14], color = :black, linestyle = :dot, linewidth = 2)

# Legend outside the plot on the right
legend!(fig[1, 2], ag, framevisible = false, tellheight = false)

fig
```

:::{.callout-tip}
## Clinical Insight

The parameter **f** (sensitive fraction) is the primary driver of treatment response:

| f value | Clinical interpretation |
|:--------|:------------------------|
| **f < 0.15** | Poor responder — most cells are resistant from the start |
| **f ≈ 0.25-0.35** | Typical responder — moderate initial response, eventual progression |
| **f > 0.50** | Good responder — deep initial response, delayed progression |
| **f > 0.85** | Excellent responder — may achieve durable complete response |

This explains why f dominates the global sensitivity analysis (Tutorial 3).
:::

## Part 5: Population Variability Preview

### Simulating Multiple Patients

Let's preview how population variability creates diverse patient responses:

```{julia}
Random.seed!(42)

# Generate a small sample of virtual patients
n_preview = 20

# Simple sampling (not using copulas yet - that's Tutorial 2)
# Just to show variability concept
preview_patients = DataFrame(
    id = 1:n_preview,
    f = clamp.(typical_f .+ randn(n_preview) .* 0.15, 0.05, 0.95),
    g = typical_g .* exp.(randn(n_preview) .* 0.5),
    k = typical_k .* exp.(randn(n_preview) .* 0.4)
)

println("Preview: $(n_preview) virtual patients")
println("\nParameter ranges:")
println("  f: $(round(minimum(preview_patients.f), digits=2)) - $(round(maximum(preview_patients.f), digits=2))")
println("  g: $(round(minimum(preview_patients.g), digits=5)) - $(round(maximum(preview_patients.g), digits=5))")
println("  k: $(round(minimum(preview_patients.k), digits=4)) - $(round(maximum(preview_patients.k), digits=4))")
```

```{julia}
#| fig-cap: "Population variability: 20 virtual patients showing diverse tumor dynamics. Green trajectories achieve complete response; gray trajectories do not. This variability motivates the need for systematic virtual population generation (Tutorial 2)."

# Build DataFrame with all patient trajectories
t_vec = collect(0:1:126)
variability_df = DataFrame()
n_responders = 0

for row in eachrow(preview_patients)
    N = [tumor_analytical(ti, row.f, row.g, row.k) for ti in t_vec]
    N_nadir = minimum(N)
    response = N_nadir < 0.14 ? "Complete Response" : "Non-Responder"
    if N_nadir < 0.14
        n_responders += 1
    end

    temp_df = DataFrame(
        time = t_vec,
        tumor_size = N,
        patient_id = string(row.id),
        response = response
    )
    append!(variability_df, temp_df)
end

# Calculate response rate for annotation
response_rate = round(100 * n_responders / n_preview, digits=1)

# Create plot using AlgebraOfGraphics
plt = data(variability_df) *
    mapping(:time => "Time (days)",
            :tumor_size => "Normalized Tumor Size",
            color = :response,
            group = :patient_id) *
    visual(Lines, linewidth = 1.5, alpha = 0.6)

# Draw the plot
fig = Figure(size = (900, 500))
ax = Axis(fig[1, 1], title = "Population Variability in Treatment Response")

ag = draw!(ax, plt, scales(Color = (; palette = [:green, :gray])))

# Add reference lines
hlines!(ax, [1.0], color = :gray, linestyle = :dash, linewidth = 1)
hlines!(ax, [0.14], color = :green, linestyle = :dot, linewidth = 2)

# Add response rate annotation in upper right of plot area
text!(ax, 90, 1.4, text = "CR rate: $(response_rate)%\n($(n_responders)/$(n_preview))",
    fontsize = 12, align = (:left, :top))

# Legend outside
legend!(fig[1, 2], ag, framevisible = false)

fig
```

:::{.callout-important}
## Looking Ahead

This preview shows why we need:

1. **Copula sampling (Tutorial 2):** The random sampling above ignores parameter correlations. In reality, patients with high f tend to have low g (r = -0.64).

2. **Calibration (Tutorial 5):** The response rate from random sampling may not match real clinical trial data.

3. **Systematic analysis:** We need to understand which parameters matter most (Tutorial 3: GSA) and whether they can be estimated (Tutorial 4: Identifiability).
:::

## Summary

### Key Concepts

1. **The Tumor Burden Model** describes cancer response to chemotherapy using two cell populations:
   - Treatment-sensitive cells (fraction f) that die at rate k
   - Treatment-resistant cells (fraction 1-f) that grow at rate g

2. **The mathematical model:**
   $$N(t) = N_0 \cdot f \cdot e^{-k \cdot t} + N_0 \cdot (1-f) \cdot e^{g \cdot t}$$

3. **Parameters from clinical data:**
   - f = 0.27 (27% sensitive), g = 0.0013 d⁻¹, k = 0.0091 d⁻¹
   - Correlation r(f, g) = -0.64

4. **Pumas model structure:**
   - `@param`: Population parameters
   - `@random`: Inter-individual variability
   - `@pre`: Parameter transformations (logit-normal for f, log-normal for g, k)
   - `@dynamics`: ODE system
   - `@derived`: Observable outputs

5. **Clinical insight:**
   - The sensitive fraction (f) is the primary driver of response
   - Population variability creates diverse outcomes

### What's Next

In **Tutorial 2: Copula Vpop Generation**, we will:

- Learn why correlated parameter sampling matters
- Understand Gaussian copulas and Sklar's theorem
- Generate realistic virtual populations preserving the r(f,g) = -0.64 correlation
- Validate that generated populations match expected distributions

### Quick Reference: Key Functions

| Function | Purpose |
|:---------|:--------|
| `@model begin ... end` | Define a Pumas model |
| `Subject(id, covariates, time)` | Create a simulation subject |
| `simobs(model, subject, params, randeffs)` | Simulate observations |
| `logit(x)` / `logistic(x)` | Bounded parameter transformations |

### References

::: {#refs}
:::
